p: SpliceKit gives superpowers to Apple's Final Cut Pro.
Click here to download!
Whilst powerful tools like CommandPost have existed for a decade, SpliceKit uses dylib code injection to get inside Final Cut Pro, giving you complete control.

SpliceKit loads a custom framework directly inside Final Cut Pro's process space giving you full access to all 78,000+ Objective-C classes and their methods through a JSON-RPC interface and MCP server.
SpliceKit contains a bunch of amazing Final Cut Pro enhancements, allowing you to do things that was never possible before with CommandPost.
Like CommandPost though, it also has a Lua-scripting environment, so you can easily code your own Lua Scripts for powerful automation.
Because SpliceKit has a MCP server, Claude Code, Codex, ChatGPT and other cloud and on-device LLMs have full control over Final Cut Pro.
SpliceKit is free and open-source, and we encourage you to contribute.
Welcome to a new world for Final Cut Pro users... 🥳
You can download the latest SpliceKit on GitHub.
You can learn more about LateNite's amazing range of apps on FCP Cafe.
SpliceKit works with Final Cut Pro v12 (lifetime/perpetual) and Final Cut Pro Creator Studio v12 (subscription).
You can find Final Cut Pro's system requirements here.
This page explains how to setup and use SpliceKit.
If you run into any problems the best place for support is the FCP Cafe Discord.
Make sure you have Final Cut Pro v12 (lifetime/perpetual) and/or Final Cut Pro Creator Studio v12 (subscription) installed.
Download the latest SpliceKit DMG from the website.
Open the DMG and drag SpliceKit into your Applications folder:
Launch SpliceKit from your Applications folder.
Pick the version of Final Cut Pro you want to use and click Continue:
SpliceKit will "patch" Final Cut Pro:
You will be prompted with:
Click Allow.
SpliceKit has now "patched" Final Cut Pro and it's ready to go!
Click Launch FCP to launch the "patched" Final Cut Pro:
Sections bar for timeline song structure, add default transition to all clips command, fuzzy search stop-word handling.
Transcript Editor Demo
Click a word to jump the playhead. Select words and press Delete to remove video segments. Drag words to reorder clips. All changes apply directly to the FCP timeline.

Powered by NVIDIA Parakeet TDT 0.6B (on-device via FluidAudio) with v3 multilingual (25 languages) as the default engine and v2 English-optimized as an option. Speaker diarization identifies who's speaking. All clips are transcribed in a single batch process for speed.

l: Command Palette — Apple Intelligence for Final Cut Pro
p: "add a cross dissolve"
"add markers every 5 seconds"
"blade at every scene change"
"color correct this clip"
"remove all the silences"
"slow this clip to half speed"
"zoom to fit the timeline"
Audio: Volume up/down, fade in/out, audio enhancements, Remove Silences (auto-detects and removes silent segments)
Built-in commands include:
Captions: Add/import captions and subtitles
Color: Color Board, Color Wheels, Color Curves, Hue/Saturation, auto-enhance, match color, balance color
Editing: Blade, delete, cut/copy/paste, trim, nudge, compound clips, detach audio, lift/overwrite, create storylines
Effects & Transitions: Browse, search, and apply any effect, transition, generator, or title by name
Export: Render, share selection, export FCPXML

Hit COMMAND+SHIFT+P inside the modded FCP to open a VS Code-style command palette with fuzzy search across 100+ editing actions. Type what you want to do in plain English and Apple Intelligence (on-device LLM via FoundationModels) translates your intent into editing actions.
Markers: Standard, to-do, and chapter markers with navigation
Multicam: Switch and cut angles 1-4, create multicam clips
Natural language examples you can ask Apple Intelligence:
Playback: Play/pause, frame stepping, go to start/end, loop, play selection, JKL-style controls
Ratings & Roles: Favorite, reject, role assignment

Remove Silences uses Apple-native AVFoundation + Accelerate (vDSP) to analyze audio and detect silent segments. No ffmpeg or external dependencies. An options panel lets you configure the detection threshold, minimum silence duration, and padding. The audio analysis runs in the background with a processing indicator, then silences are bladed and ripple-deleted from the timeline.

Scene Detection uses histogram-based frame comparison via the Accelerate framework's vImage to detect cuts and shot changes. Configurable sensitivity and sample interval. Markers are inserted programmatically at exact timecodes — no playhead movement required — making it fast even on long sequences.
Scene Detection: Analyze video for shot boundaries and automatically add markers or blade at every scene change
Speed: Normal, 2x/4x/8x/20x fast, 50%/25%/10% slow, reverse, freeze frame, hold frame
The AI understands your intent, maps it to the right sequence of FCP actions, and executes them — all without leaving the keyboard.
Transform: Crop, distort, reframe, stabilize
View: Zoom to fit, snapping, skimming, timeline index, inspector

c:
splicekit> version
splicekit> classes FFAnchored
splicekit> methods FFPlayer
splicekit> props FFAnchoredSequence
splicekit> super FFAnchoredSequence
splicekit> ivars FFLibrary

p: First, create a Python virtual environment for the MCP server and install the mcp package:
Use the virtual environment's Python as the MCP command.
Point MCP at the server script inside the installed SpliceKit app bundle:
The MCP server connects to the SpliceKit bridge running inside Final Cut Pro on 127.0.0.1:9876, so the modded Final Cut Pro must be running.

c:
python3 -m venv ~/.venvs/splicekit-mcp
~/.venvs/splicekit-mcp/bin/python -m pip install --upgrade pip mcp

p: If you run into any bugs, crashes, or you have a feature request, you can post an issue here.
If you have any general questions or feedback, you can discuss here.
GitHub registration is free, and this GitHub repository is actively monitored by LateNite.
You can also email us: support@latenitefilms.com
We're based in Melbourne, Australia - you can check the timezone here.
A Command Palette inside Final Cut Pro (press COMMAND+SHIFT+P) where you type what you want
A Python scripting interface if you want to write your own automation
A Transcript Panel for text-based editing
Adding markers at every beat of a song? Click... click... click...
AI Editing -- describe what you want in plain English and let an AI assistant do the work
An AI assistant (like Claude) that can run editing commands for you

And because SpliceKit is a plugin framework, new features get added all the time. You can even create your own plugins with the help of AI -- describe what you want a plugin to do, and Claude can help you build it.
Applying the same color grade to 50 clips? Hope you like copy-paste.
Batch Export -- export every clip on your timeline individually, with all effects baked in
Beat Detection -- finds beats in music so you can sync edits to the rhythm
Command Palette (Cmd+Shift+P) -- search and run any editing command by typing, instead of hunting through menus
Final Cut Pro is a powerful video editor, but everything in it is manual. You click, drag, scrub, hunt through menus. That's fine for some tasks, but painful for others:
Here's some of what's already built in:
Here's the non-technical version:
How Does It Work?
It can now see and control everything FCP does -- the timeline, your clips, effects, playback, all of it.
It makes a separate copy of Final Cut Pro in your home folder and adds a small plugin (a dylib) to it.
It's also a plugin framework - so you can build addons and plugins to do all kinds of awesome things that you couldn't normally do with FCP plugins.
OK, But What Does That Actually Mean?
Removing silences from an hour-long interview? That's hundreds of manual cuts.
Scene Detection -- automatically finds every cut/scene change in your footage
Silence Removal -- automatically detects and removes dead air from interviews and podcasts
Social Media Captions -- auto-generate word-by-word animated captions (TikTok/Reels style)
SpliceKit is really a collection of plugins that already adds a ton of quality-of-life features to FCP out of the box, plus an AI framework and plugin system that makes it easy to add even more.
SpliceKit lets you control Final Cut Pro with text commands and AI instead of clicking buttons and dragging things around.
SpliceKit patches your copy of Final Cut Pro.
Think of it like this: Final Cut Pro is a car with only a steering wheel and pedals. SpliceKit adds voice control and autopilot.

Transcript Editor -- transcribes your footage, shows you the words, and lets you edit your video by editing the text. Delete a sentence? The video gets cut too. Drag words around? The clips move to match.
When you launch the patched Final Cut Pro, SpliceKit wakes up inside it.
You talk to SpliceKit through text. There are a few ways:
Your original Final Cut Pro stays untouched.
Being honest:
DMCA Section 1201(f) (US law) -- you're allowed to reverse-engineer software to make it work with other programs
EU Software Directive Article 6 -- same principle in Europe
Future Final Cut Pro updates could break compatibility. When Apple updates Final Cut Pro, SpliceKit may need to be re-patched. This is an inconvenience, not a danger.
Is it legal?

It disables Final Cut Pro's sandbox (the security boundary that limits what FCP can access on your system). This is required for the plugin to work. In practice, this just means the patched Final Cut Pro has the same file access as any other app on your Mac.
It uses internal Final Cut Pro APIs that Apple doesn't document. This means some features might behave unexpectedly on edge cases. The undo button is your friend.
Many popular Mac apps work the same way under the hood (BetterTouchTool, accessibility tools, etc.).
No. SpliceKit makes a copy of Final Cut Pro in ~/Applications/SpliceKit/ and patches the copy.

No. SpliceKit runs entirely on your local machine. It doesn't modify Apple's servers, bypass DRM on content, or do anything online. It's the software equivalent of opening the hood of your own car and adding a turbocharger - Apple doesn't monitor or restrict what you run locally.
SpliceKit is MIT licensed (open source, free to use and modify).
That said, SpliceKit does perform real edits when you ask it to. Just like any editing tool, you can undo (COMMAND+Z) if something goes wrong, and you should keep backups of important projects.
This is the question everyone asks, so let's break it down:
What are the actual risks?
Will Apple ban my account?
Will it break my Mac or my projects?
Yes. Reverse engineering for interoperability is explicitly protected under:
Your original Final Cut Pro from the App Store is never modified. Your libraries, projects, and media files are not changed by the patching process.
Everyone who uses Final Cut Pro.
Seriously. You don't need to be a programmer or a power user to benefit from SpliceKit. Most of the built-in features are things that every editor will appreciate:
Casual editors - silence removal, transcript editing, and the command palette make everyday editing faster and less tedious
Professional editors - batch export, scene detection, beat sync, and caption generation save hours on real projects
Content creators - auto-generate TikTok/Reels-style captions, quickly cut down long recordings, batch-process clips for social media
People who want AI-assisted editing - describe edits in plain English and let an AI handle the repetitive work for you

Developers and tinkerers - build your own plugins in Python, Lua, or with AI assistance. The plugin framework gives you access to everything inside Final Cut Pro, so you can create tools that Apple's official plugin system doesn't support

The built-in plugins already handle the most common pain points. But if you ever think "I wish Final Cut Pro could do X" - SpliceKit probably makes it possible, and AI can help you build it even if you've never written a line of code.
Apologies in advance if you have any trouble using SpliceKit.
Please read this page first, and if the solutions here don't solve your problem, please post an issue on GitHub and we'll do our best to solve it.

l: Malware Block and Moved to Trash
p: We've had a couple of users report seeing this error:
SpliceKit isn't Malware - you can look at all the source code to confirm.
To resolve this warning right-click on SpliceKit and select Get Info, then untick Override Malware Protection:
SpliceKit should now launch correctly.
SpliceKit is open source and anyone can contribute to it.
In a world of large language models (LLMs), it's easier than ever.
Click the Current Branch button then New Branch:
Download the free GitHub Desktop.
GitHub Desktop will ask you if you How are you planning to use this fork?, just leave it as To contribute to the parent project and click Continue:
GitHub is where people build software. More than 150 million people use GitHub to discover, fork, and contribute to over 420 million projects.
Happy Coding!
If you click over to the History tab, you can see all the code changes:
If you don't already have a free GitHub Account, please create one.
In GitHub Desktop choose where you want to save your local files, then click Clone:
In the Owner dropdown, select your GitHub username, then click Create fork.
Now click the green Code button, then Open with GitHub Desktop:
On Safari you'll be presented with a warning, click Allow:
Once installed, go to the SpliceKit GitHub Repository.
Once you have an account, back on the SpliceKit GitHub Repository, click the Fork button:

The LLM will recognise that you're in a GitHub fork, and can help you commit code (i.e save it to the GitHub Cloud) and make a Pull Request (i.e. submit your code to the wider SpliceKit project for review).
Type a name then click Create Branch:
You can now point your preferred LLM, such as Claude Code or Codex to the folder containing your code, and let it go nuts.
You now have the SpliceKit code on your local machine!
You now have your own Fork of SpliceKit! 🥳
🎉 Released:
14th April 2026
🐞 Bug Fix:
Fix transcript editor missing connected clips (FFAnchoredClip URL resolution), mixer panel, caption enhancements, CloudContent diagnostic logging, patcher launch monitoring.
🎉 Released:
14th April 2026
🐞 Bug Fix:
Fix CloudKit crash on Final Cut Pro 12.2+, patcher diagnostic logging to ~/Library/Logs/SpliceKit/patcher.log, comprehensive startup diagnostics in dylib, disable spring-loaded blade.
🎉 Released:
14th April 2026
🐞 Bug Fixes:

Fix patcher signing on macOS 15.7.3 — the patcher was applying only 1 entitlement (cs-disable-library-validation) instead of the full set needed for ad-hoc signing. This caused the re-signed app to launch unsigned on some systems, triggering a CloudKit crash (EXC_BREAKPOINT in CloudContentCatalog.updateCatalogAndRegistry()). Now applies all 4 required entitlements including explicit app-sandbox=false to prevent CloudKit entitlement validation failures. Fixed across all patcher paths: GUI app (initial patch + update), shell script, and entitlements.plist. (Fixes #23)
📄 Documentation:
Added plain-English explainer — What Is SpliceKit? covers what it is, how it works, and whether it's safe, written for non-technical users. Linked from the top of the README.
🎉 Released:
14th April 2026
🐞 Bug Fixes:
Transcript persistence across projects — switching projects no longer shows stale transcript from previous project; added transcript.clear RPC endpoint
addTodoMarker fixed — was returning "No responder handled"; now uses direct sequence method
Silence detection — was returning 0 silences on clips with obvious pauses; improved threshold algorithm and added start-to-start interval detection
Timeline duration — was reporting 0.000s; now falls back to summing spine clip durations
add_markers_at_times offset — now accounts for timeline start offset (e.g. 01:00:00:00)
🥳 New Features:
OpenTimelineIO import/export — universal timeline exchange with DaVinci Resolve, Premiere Pro, Avid (.otio, .edl, .aaf)
Native OTIO-to-FCPXML converter — frame-exact conversion with canonical SMPTE timebases
Compound clip drilling — get_timeline_clips now exposes isCompound, nestedItemCount, and include_nested parameter
Batch editing tools — apply_transition_to_all_clips, batch_apply_effect, batch_color_correct
Expanded command palette — full categorized action lists, comprehensive AI capabilities prompt
Lua example — shuffle_clips.lua script

6 built-in tools: edit (timeline actions), seek (playhead positioning), clips (read timeline state), repeat_action (batch operations like "blade every 5 seconds"), effect (apply effects), menu (execute any Final Cut Pro menu command)
8 April 2026
8 built-in menu scripts (timeline report, remove silences, blade every 5s, balance color, cross dissolves, generate captions, export XML, screenshot viewer)
Apple Intelligence+ (Agentic Mode):

Apple Intelligence+ can read your timeline, seek to specific times, blade/cut clips, add markers, apply effects, execute menu commands, and repeat actions at intervals — all from a natural language prompt
Auto-transcribe: generates captions without manual transcription step
Bridge API: set_bridge_option_value(defaultSpatialConformType, fill)
Caption script is now non-blocking for long clips with transcript reuse
Default Spatial Conform Type (PR #22):
Drop shadow on all caption text for readability
Embedded Lua 5.4 VM running directly in FCP's process (zero latency)
Engine preference persists across sessions via NSUserDefaults
Fix caption engine override: caption generation now uses the user's configured transcription engine instead of forcing FCP Native
Fix Lua RPC main thread deadlock: sk.rpc() no longer forces every call through main thread, preventing soft deadlocks during polling loops
Fix parakeet-transcriber build for updated FluidAudio API (AsrManager.initialize → loadModels, new model version enum variants)
Live coding: save .lua files to ~/Library/Application Support/SpliceKit/lua/auto/
Lower-third positioning via Motion channel hierarchy
Lua Scripting Engine:
MCP tools: lua_execute, lua_execute_file, lua_reset, lua_watch, lua_state
Menu: Enhancements Options Default Spatial Conform
New Apple Intelligence+ engine in the Command Palette — an agentic AI mode powered by Apple's on-device FoundationModels framework with tool-calling capabilities
Options panel dropdown + Command Palette cycling
Override default spatial conform (Fit/Fill/None) for new timeline clips
reloadMicaDocument fix: no more greyed-out text or Inspector crashes
REPL panel (CONTROL+OPTION+L) for interactive scripting
Screen freeze hides temp project switch (seamless UX)
sk module with 25+ actions: sk.blade(), sk.seek(), sk.clips(), sk.rpc(), sk.eval()
Smart pattern detection: phrases like "cut every 3 seconds" or "add markers every 10 seconds" are intercepted for fast direct execution
Social Captions (Improved):
Three AI engines selectable from the Command Palette dropdown: Apple Intelligence (basic), Gemma 4 (local MLX), and Apple Intelligence+ (agentic with tools) — Apple Intelligence+ is now the default
Timeline-aware context: Apple Intelligence+ automatically knows your project name, duration, clip count, frame rate, and playhead position
Uses Final Cut Pro's built-in Basic Title — works on all Final Cut Pro installations, no custom templates needed
Word-by-word karaoke highlighting via per-word text-style colouring

To uninstall SpliceKit, simply drag the application to the trash/bin.
SpliceKit has been created by Brian Elliott Tate.
SpliceKit is supported by LateNite, who also created CommandPost, Transfer Toolbox, BRAW Toolbox, Marker Toolbox, Gyroflow Toolbox and many other apps for professional video editors.
You can learn more about LateNite's creations over at FCP Cafe.
This is the privacy policy for the SpliceKit, macOS Application that is available on GitHub.
SpliceKit does not collect any user data. No personal information is transmitted, stored, or used by this application.
SpliceKit does not use any third-party analytics or tracking tools. It does not contain any in-app purchases or advertisements.
We use this GitHub Repository for documentation hosting and technical support. You can read GitHub's Privacy Policy here.
If you have any questions or concerns, you can submit an issue, or email us: support@latenitefilms.com
